narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated an 87% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows you ranked in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your LSAT score of 19.3 is also well under the average of around 37 that successful students usually have, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 lags behind the dataset average of 3.21. Coming from a middle-income family puts you right around the norm, while being an Asian male planning full-time studies aligns closely with the majority of applicants who go on to pass. When the model weighed all this, your third-year decile ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, more than any other factor. Right behind it, your low LSAT score reinforced that risk significantly. Your first-year decile came next in influence, pushing the prediction further in the same direction, followed by family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender, each adding a bit more weight toward the unfavorable outcome. Compared to typical students who pass—often with stronger early grades, higher test scores, and balanced profiles—yours stood out as riskier across several key academic markers. The good news is some of these can change: retaking and boosting your LSAT could have a big impact, as it's one of the top influencers and something you can prepare for directly. Working to improve your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program might help shift future predictions, since undergrad performance played a notable role too. Even though past school deciles are fixed, demonstrating recent academic growth in any new applications could signal improvement to the model. Focus on these steps, and you could strengthen your profile enough to tip the odds toward passing the bar and getting approved next time. Hang in there—many students turn things around with targeted effort.
